nodes.speaker: june
lines: Oh. The light guy.
Come in, if you're coming. Mind the boxes.

8: (She's watching the door before it's open.)

choices.text: How are you holding up?
(Set the groceries on the counter.)
Tell me about your mother.
Cold's coming in.
We should talk about staying out of sight.
Where do you want to be, when the town goes dark?
There's a form. A way to make you count.
(Just sit with her a while.)
(Chat a while.)
I've got rounds. Back later.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: You've got time to loiter? Bold, for the man in charge of turning off the sun.
Fine. Pick a subject. Nothing heavier than the weather unless I start it.

choices.text: Any word on the car?
What do you make of this town?
You think the job's a joke, don't you.
That red light on your ceiling at night —
The plant across the bay.
You've been listening to the train.
(Let Gigi hop up in her lap.)
(Back.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Honestly? I'm sorting a dead woman's summer clothes into piles nobody wants. So. Peak of my career.
I'm fine. I'm doing the thing where you say fine and it counts.

choices.text: You don't have to do the fine thing with me.
Fine's a good number. Stay there.
(Change the subject.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: ...I keep finding her handwriting. Grocery lists. A note that just says 'ask about the gutters.'
You can throw out a person's whole life and it's the gutters that get you.
Anyway. That's the report. Fine, plus gutters.

choices.text: For what it's worth, I'm glad you're still here.
Tell me about her.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: (You sit. She keeps folding. Outside, the wind does something to the reeds.)
This is the most anyone's sat still in this house since August.
Don't make it weird. It's not weird. Keep it not weird.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: It's September. It's supposed to be warm through the boat show.
My mother had a rule: no sweaters till the fair. I've been in a sweater since Tuesday.

6: It got cold fast. Too fast.
The radiator knocks and then it just... stops. Like it thought better of it.
8: It's not cold. It's quiet. There's a difference and I didn't used to know it.

choices.text: The town closes early. Always has.
(Say nothing about the reason.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: My car is still 'waiting on a part.' Three weeks. Every shop in the county, same part, same wait.
I'm starting to think the part is me.

6: The car's still waiting on a part. I stopped calling.
You call enough times, they start saying your name too kindly.
8: Nobody's fixing the car. I know that now. I don't think it was ever the car.

choices.text: (Let the joke land and leave it.)
It's not the car, June.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Everyone here is so nice it's like being frosted. I keep waiting to find the cake.
The bookstore lady gave me a novel and wouldn't take a dollar. The diner woman knows my order and I've been in twice.

choices.text: That's just how it is off-season.
Careful who clocks your order.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: So your actual job is to turn a town off. Like a porch light.
Pull the swim lines, drain the pool, kill the lamps one by one. That's a life?

choices.text: It's the family trade. My uncle did it before me.
Somebody stays. Might as well be the one who grew up here.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: You can see the power plant from the back window. Those two domes. Little red lights that never blink out.
It's stupid, but it's the one thing out there that stays on all night. I've started looking for it. Like a nightlight.

8: The plant's still lit. Every window in town goes dark and that thing just... hums.
I don't find it comforting anymore. I don't know when that changed.

choices.text: The old-timers say it's rude. Never closing.
(Leave the plant out of it.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: There's a lighthouse on the point. You can see the beam sweep across the ceiling in here at night.
Red. Slow. My mother used to say it was the town breathing.
That's the kind of thing you say to a kid so she'll go to sleep and not ask what the light's for.

choices.text: It's just an old harbor light.
(Don't tell her what it's for.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: You brought the cat. The tuxedo one from the bookstore.
(She lets it into her lap without asking. It settles like it lives here.)
Okay. This one's allowed. This one can stay as long as it wants.

choices.text: Her name's Gigi. She adopted me.
She stares at the corners sometimes. Ignore it.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: (You set the bag down. Bread. Eggs. The good coffee she pretended not to want.)
You didn't have to do that.

choices.text: It's just groceries. Eat something.
You shouldn't have to get by on scraps. Not alone.
The diner does a double order. Nobody blinks.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: ...Thank you. That's a normal amount of thank you, don't read into it.
I'll pay you back. When the car's fixed. When there's a bank that's open. When any of this makes sense.
Put the eggs by the window, it's the only cold shelf that works.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Don't. Don't do the voice.
I'm not a stray you found in the rain. I had a whole life on Tuesday. I have a lease and a dentist.
Bring the bread. Skip the sympathy. It curdles faster than the milk.

choices.text: Understood. Just the bread.
(Nod. Let it go.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: The diner woman. Roz. She notices the double order?
God. I've been getting someone in trouble by eating.
Tell me what to do. I'll eat less. I'll eat air.

choices.text: You'll eat. I'll handle Roz. Just be careful with the lights.
Don't buy anything yourself. Let me carry it.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: My mother. Where do you even start with a person you're only meeting through her junk drawer.

choices.text: The cottage. She kept it all these years?
Was there a boat? A little one?
I found your mother's dinghy.
You've been sorting her things.
What was she like in summer?
(Let it rest.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: She bought it the year I was born. Gray shingles, bad plumbing, a view of the marsh she called 'the good side.'
We came every July. Then it was every other July. Then I was busy and it was never.
She stopped telling me when the gutters needed doing. I found that note today. Twenty years too late to help.

choices.text: You came back. That counts.
(Just listen.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: In summer she was somebody else. Louder. She'd yell at the Sound like it owed her money.
She swam every morning past the second buoy, which you're not supposed to do, which she knew.
The town loved her. That's the part I can't square. They all loved her and none of them called me when she got sick.

choices.text: This town keeps its own kind of quiet.
They should have called.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: There was a dinghy. Green, peeling, 'CARROW' on the transom in nail polish because she lost the paint.
It's not on the rack. I went down to look and the slot's just... empty.
I had a stupid plan, the first week. Row myself out to the point, flag the ferry. Then the boat was gone too. Like the town filed it away.

choices.text: I'll look for it. That's half my job, counting boats.
(Say nothing about the harbor log.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: You found it. Her dinghy. Where.
(You tell her. Racked wrong, two slips over, oars stowed like someone tidied.)
...She'd have hated that. She never stowed an oar right in her life.

choices.text: It's seaworthy. If it ever comes to that.
It stays put for now. Rowing out is how people get noticed.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Three piles. Keep, donate, and the one I can't name.
Her reading glasses go in the third pile. A ticket stub. A single earring she kept for forty years like the other one might swim back.
You'd think grief would be big. It's this. It's an earring and a decision you keep not making.

choices.text: You don't have to finish it this week.
What's the hardest thing in the pile?

nodes.speaker: june
lines: A photo. Her and me on the seawall, I'm maybe six, we're both squinting.
On the back she wrote the date and then, later, in different pen: 'the good year.'
I don't know which year she meant. I've decided not to find out. Some things you're allowed to leave closed.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: I came here to close a house. I did not sign up for the house closing around me.
But if I leave before it's done, she's just... unfinished. Boxes in a cold room forever.
So I stay. And I sort. And the town gets quieter, and I tell myself that's grief. It's grief, right?

choices.text: (Tell her the truth about the quiet.)
It's grief. Rest.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: One more room. The bedroom. I keep saving it for a day I feel stronger.
That day's not coming, is it. You just do it on a Tuesday and cry into a pillowcase you then have to decide about.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Nope. Not today. She's staying in the boxes today.
Ask me something with a smaller answer.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Out of sight. You keep saying that like it's weather.
Say the real sentence. Why does a summer person closing up a house have to hide from a nice little town?

choices.text: Just stay inside. Stay down. Can you do that for me?
You have to stay hidden. This isn't a game.
Because they take a count. And you're not on it.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: A count. Like a census.
(You give her as much as you can carry and no more. She watches your face do the math.)
You're not telling me all of it. I can see the door you're keeping shut. Fine. Keep it shut. For now.

choices.text: The less you know, the safer you sound to them.
I'll tell you everything. Just not tonight.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Okay. Okay, I trust you. God help me, I actually do.
I'll stay down. Lights off after your rounds, no radio, no cooking after dark. I've read a book, I know the drill.
Here. The spare key. So you can come and go without me being a jumpy idiot at the door.
You tell me hide, I hide. But you come back. That's the deal. You always come back.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: No. Not until you look at me and tell me what I'm hiding from.
I'm not going to sit in a dead woman's dark house because you've got a feeling and a keyring.
Give me a reason or I'm walking into that diner tomorrow and asking somebody who'll actually answer.

choices.text: You do that and it's over. For both of us. Please.
You're right. You deserve better than my nerves.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: 'For both of us.' That's the first true thing you've said. It's costing you something too.
...Fine. I'll stay down. Not because I'm scared. Because you are, and you don't scare easy, and that scares me.
But you owe me the whole story. When it's time. Don't make me find it in a boat log.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: So we agree. I keep living like a person until you can tell me why not.
I'll be smart about the lights. That much I'll give you. But I'm not a secret yet. I'm just careful.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Go do your rounds, light guy. Turn the town off.
I'll be here. Being furniture. Very quiet, very unremarkable furniture.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: You want to know where I'll be. The last night. When you shut it all down.
That's a strange question to ask a person so gently. Where do you want me, Wren?

choices.text: The church. Amaral keeps it unlocked. You won't be alone.
The old duck blind in the marsh. Nobody goes out there.
Right here. Your mother's cottage. Lights out, stay down.
My house. The Neck. With me.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: The church. The priest with the sad eyes and the bell he rings at the wrong hours.
He looked at me once like he already knew. Didn't say a word. Just held the door.
Okay. The church. If I have to be still, I'd rather be still where somebody's ringing something.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: The blind. Cold, wet, and mine, at least. Nobody's tidied an oar out there in years.
Just me and the reeds and whatever the tide's doing.
...Say it's the right call. I need you to say it, even if you're guessing.

choices.text: It's the right call.
I'm guessing. But I'd bet you on it.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Here. Of course here. Where else does a Carrow wait out a bad night.
I'll sit in her chair with the lights off and pretend the sweep of that red beam is her breathing.
That's not comforting when I say it out loud. Let's pretend it is.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Your house. On the Neck. Where the neighbors notice things.
You know what you're offering. You'd be putting me under your own roof, on your own count. That's not hiding. That's a flag planted.
...Yeah. Take me home. I'm tired of being a secret in somebody else's kitchen.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: So. You're the one who saw the light.
Public Works, the badge says. You turn a whole town off for the winter. That's a real job somebody has?
I'd offer you coffee but I'm rationing it against a car that's never getting fixed.

choices.text: You really shouldn't still be here.
It's a good job. Quiet. You get used to the water.
Your mother's cottage. I'm sorry for your loss.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: I know I shouldn't be here. Believe me, I've read the room. Everything's closing. The beach 'closed.' The pool's a hole.
But my car won't start, the ferry ran its last, and I am not leaving a job half-done in that bedroom.
So unless you're here to physically carry me to a bus, I'm finishing. Politely. Out of everyone's way.

choices.text: Then stay out of sight while you finish. Really out of sight.
Fair. I'll keep an eye out for you.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: 'Used to the water.' You people talk about the Sound like it's a coworker.
It's pretty here. Pretty and empty and getting emptier by the hour. I keep expecting a tumbleweed made of hydrangeas.
You're not going to tell me to leave, are you. Everybody else does it with their eyebrows.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Thank you. That's the first time somebody's said it plainly instead of bringing a casserole and a face.
She loved this town. That's the part I keep tripping on. It loved her back and it's being so strange to me.
Anyway. You didn't come to watch me leak. What do you want, light guy?

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Here's where we are. You're the first person in this town who talks to me like I'm a person and not a scheduling problem.
I don't know yet if that makes you the good one or just the useful one. Jury's out.
Come back, though. Nobody else does.

choices.text: Tell me about your mother while I'm here.
We should talk about keeping you unnoticed.
I'll be back tomorrow.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Unnoticed. You said that with real weight for a man describing a beach town in October.
I'm not going to hide, exactly. I've done nothing. But I'll grant you this: I'll keep the front lights off after dark.
Small town. People talk. I get it. I don't want to be the thing they talk about.

choices.text: That's all I'm asking. For now.
(You want to say more. You don't. Not yet.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: You've got that look. You found something on your rounds.
(You tell her. The green dinghy, her mother's, racked two slips over with the oars stowed too neat.)
So it didn't wander off. Somebody moved it. Somebody in this town put my mother's boat away and didn't say a word.

choices.text: Boats get shuffled during Closing. It's probably nothing.
I don't think it's nothing either. Be careful.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Day four of my little residency. I've done the kitchen, the porch, and half a linen closet.
The town's noticeably thinner. There were kids on bikes on Monday. Now it's just gulls and your truck.
Tell me it's normal for a place to empty out this fast. Lie convincingly if you have to.

choices.text: It's normal. Everyone drains out after Labor Day.
It's normal. It just feels like more when you're the one left.
Keep the coffee. Careful with the double orders, though.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Okay. I'm done pretending this is quaint.
The train went by last night and didn't stop. The board on the platform was just... blank. Not delayed. Blank.
The Low-Tide people stand at the water at the wrong hours now. The bookstore cats won't come near the marsh side.
This isn't a sleepy town, Wren. Sleepy towns don't get quieter on a schedule. Tell me I'm wrong.

choices.text: (Tell her the truth. She's earned it.)
You're tired and grieving. It's playing tricks.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: You're not laughing it off. You're not managing me. You're just... standing there letting it be true.
That's worse. That's so much worse and also the first honest thing anyone's given me since I got here.
How bad. Give me the shape of it, not the whole thing. Just the shape.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Tricks. Right. My grief rescheduled the Amtrak.
You know what, forget it. I'll ask the frosted people. Somebody in this town owes me a straight answer and it's clearly not the guy with the keys.
...Sorry. That was mean. You just — you had a chance to be honest and you picked the pillow.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: So it's real. Whatever it is. It's real and it's coming and the whole town's just... setting the table for it.
And they're kind about it. That's the thing I can't hold. They bring you casseroles. They know, and they bring casseroles.
I'm scared, Wren. Not the fun creepy-cottage scared. The other kind. I need you to not disappear on me.

choices.text: I'm not going anywhere. I found you. That means something.
I don't have all the answers. But I'm not leaving you to it.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: The neighbors stopped waving. Did you notice? Two days ago they waved. Now they just... look, a beat too long, and go inside.
It's not hostile. That's what gets me. It's gentle. Like they're already a little sorry.
Okay. New rules. Tell me what to do and I'll do it. I'm out of clever.

choices.text: Stay down. Stay quiet. Trust me and only me.
Give me a day. I'm working on something better than hiding.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: You hear the horn now and there's a gap. A wrong little gap. Like the sound arrives and the train forgets to.
I counted last night. Horn, crossing bells, and then nothing crossed. I watched the whole gap happen.
I used to take that train home. That's the part that hollows me out. I used to just... take it home.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: The meter on the side of the house is spinning. I haven't run anything but a kettle.
And you came by today with a clipboard and a face. Meter day, right? You said it last week without meaning to.
This is a counting day. I can feel it. What happens if the count finds me?

choices.text: (Tell her exactly what the count is for.)
Nothing happens. I log the reading and you stay off the paper.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: (You tell her. The Ledger. Two hundred names by household. What the town does when the light goes out, in the plainest words you have.)
...A manifest. Like a ship's crew. And anyone aboard who isn't on it is a stowaway.
You could have lied. You've been half-lying to protect me for a week and today, of all days, you didn't.
Thank you. I mean it in the biggest way the word comes in.

choices.text: You should know what you're standing in. It's your name too.
I won't put your reading in the book. Whatever it costs.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: 'Off the paper.' You keep giving me the tidy version.
I'll take it, because I'm tired and I trust your hands even when I don't trust your sentences.
But there's a paper, Wren. There's always a paper. And someday you're going to have to read me the whole thing.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: So the reading. When you log it. What are you going to write?
No — don't tell me the number. I don't want to be a number I have to remember.
Just make it the right kind of lie. The kind that keeps me breathing till you think of something real.

choices.text: I'll zero it. Cleanly. My uncle taught me how.
I'll handle it. Trust the hands, not the sentence.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Okay. Do your thing with the meter. I'll be inside not existing.
And Wren — after. Come tell me it worked. Even if you have to knock our little knock. I'll be up.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: I've made up my mind and you're not going to talk me out of it, so save the keyring.
The last ferry's today. I'm going to walk down to the slip in broad daylight and get on it like a normal person with a normal life.
You've been kind. Kind and evasive. That's not enough to stake my breathing on. I'm going while there's still a boat.

choices.text: June — the ferry isn't what you think it is. Please wait.
(You can't hold her. Let her go and follow.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: I'm sorry. I am. But sitting in a dark house waiting to be counted is not a plan, it's a coffin with tea.
Don't follow too close. If it goes wrong I don't want it going wrong for both of us.
(She's already got her coat. She's already out the door and down the lane before you can find the sentence that stops her.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: You knocked our knock last night and then you didn't say anything for a full minute.
So it's done. The count. You wrote me out of it.
Tell me what that cost you. Don't do the 'nothing' thing. I can read a bill.

choices.text: (Tell her what forging the count means for you.)
It cost what it cost. You're still here. That's the math I wanted.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: (You tell her. The clean zero. The false line. What it means that the census is wrong now, and that you made it wrong on purpose, for her.)
You put your name on a lie so mine wouldn't have to be true.
I don't have anything the size of that to give back. Just — I see it. Whatever happens, I saw what you did.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: The ferry left this afternoon. I heard the horn from the window.
A week ago that would've gutted me. Today I just thought: good. One less way for me to lose my nerve.
The way out's gone. Which means the only way through is you and me and whatever you've got left in that keyring.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: So. No boat, no train, no car with a part. I'm all in on Lantern Neck, apparently. Terrible town, wouldn't recommend, can't leave.
Whatever you're cooking up to save me — start telling me the real version. I'm done being protected by omission.

choices.text: There may be a way to make you count. Officially. Let me work it.
Give me one more day. Then you get all of it.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: The ferry goes today. I keep waiting to feel the pull to run for it. It's not coming.
That should scare me more than it does. When did I decide to stay in the town that's trying to subtract me.
Around when you started knocking, probably. Don't let it go to your head.

choices.text: The ferry isn't the way out you think. You're safer here, with me.
(Say nothing. Just stay a while.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Don't. Don't say 'I told you.' I know. I walked down there and the slip was wrong and the boat was wrong and the ten kind faces were the worst part.
You came and got me. In front of everyone. That's on your count now too, isn't it.
...I'm still shaking. Sit down. Tell me it can still be fixed. Lie a little if you have to, I've lowered my standards.

choices.text: It can still be fixed. And this time I mean it.
You're alive and you're hidden. We build from there.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: You've got a piece of paper in your hand and you've been holding it like it might bite.
Out with it, Wren. Whatever this is, you've clearly decided it's the thing. So decide it out loud.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: (You lay it out. Form 12-C. Amendment to Household Composition. A way for the count to be right with her in it — not hidden, claimed.)
Claimed. Like luggage. Like a dependent. Somebody signs a line and I become... theirs. Part of a household. Part of the town.
That's not a hiding place. That's a life sentence in a place I came to bury my mother. You understand what you're asking.

choices.text: I understand. It's enormous. It's also the only way you get to keep breathing and be free.
What does being 'entered' actually mean?
(Give her room. Don't push.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Free. In a town I can never leave, on a line in a book I can never come off of. Your definitions need work.
But. If the other option is a dark house and a count that puts me over the side — yeah. Freedom's relative. I hear you.
Whose name goes next to mine, though. The diner woman? The book witch? The sad priest? ...You? I need a face before I hand it my life.

choices.text: It's arranged. A household's already agreed to take you. It's real.
I'll get you a household. Roz, Edith, the priest — or me. Just say yes.
Take the night. But this is the way through. Say it back when you can.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Entered. You keep using the word like it's warm.
(You tell her about the book. Two hundred names. And one blank line at the bottom of every year's page. Always exactly one.)
One blank line. Every year. Waiting.
...That's the most frightening kindness I've ever heard described. Somebody's been leaving a seat out. For a very long time.

choices.text: The seat's real. It could be yours. That's the whole offer.
(Let the weight of it sit.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Give me a second. This is my whole life you're asking me to sign into a town that files boats away.
Yesterday my plan was 'don't get counted.' Now the plan is 'get counted so hard it becomes a home.' That's a lot of turn for one week.
...Ask me again. Plainly. I think I need to hear you actually ask.

choices.text: June. Let me put your name in the book. Stay. Be one of us.
Sleep on it. The line's not going anywhere. It never has.
If it's too much, we hide you instead. Your name stays yours. Your call.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Okay.
Okay. Yes. Put me in the book. Enter me. Make me one of the two hundred and — what, one. Two hundred and one.
My mother would think I'd lost my mind. Or she'd think I finally came home. Some days those were the same sentence with her.
Do it, Wren. Before I get clever again. Sign me aboard.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: So that's it. Fresh ink on the blank line. I'm on the manifest.
It doesn't feel like safety. It feels like getting married to a lighthouse. But it's yours, and I chose it, and that's more than I've been able to say about anything since June.
Go file it. Then come back and tell me I did the brave thing and not the stupid one.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: No. Not this. I can't bind myself to this place forever on a night's notice, even to live.
Hide me. Take the risk. If I'm going to gamble my name I'll gamble it on getting to keep it as just mine.
Don't look like that. You gave me a door. I'm allowed to pick which one.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: They're boarding up Main Street. I watched from the marsh side. Neighbors with drills, very tidy, very quiet.
And the bell's going at odd hours. The sad priest. It's the only sound in town that's trying to reach me instead of hush me.
It's the last real day, isn't it. Whatever you're going to do, it's got to be tomorrow. So. What are we doing.

choices.text: We're getting you counted, the right way. Trust me one more day.
Tonight there's a vigil at the church. The Watch. Stay put through it.
We need to pick where you'll be when I douse the light.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: The Watch. The whole town at the church, facing the water, while the priest rings the hours.
'So no one faces the water alone' — that's what he told me, when he thought I was just a sad girl at the back pew.
I'll stay down through it. But Wren — that bell's for me too now, isn't it. Whatever's out there, he's ringing it at me too.

choices.text: He's ringing it for everyone the water might take. Including you.
(Don't answer that. Some kindnesses you don't explain.)

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Last day. And I'm on the book. A resident of Lantern Neck, ink barely dry, already regretting the winters.
You're going up to the point tonight to turn off the last light. And then... the town crosses over. And I cross with it. Because my name's aboard.
I'm not going to hide tonight. That's the whole point, isn't it. I don't have to. Say it back to me so I believe it.

choices.text: You don't have to hide. You're counted. You're one of us.
Leave the lights on tonight. You've earned the right to be seen.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: So it's tonight. You climb the point, you flip the switch, the town goes dark and something comes ashore to check the list. And I'm not on it.
I'll be where we said. Lights off, no kettle, breathing through my sleeve.
Come find me after. If there's an after. Knock our knock. I'll count the seconds till you do.

choices.text: Wherever you're hiding, I'll be thinking of it the whole climb.
Stay down no matter what you hear. Especially what you hear.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: One more thing. If it doesn't go well tonight — if I'm the name that comes up short —
You did more for a stranger with a dead mother than this whole town did for a woman it loved. Don't you dare carry it like a debt.
Now go. Turn the town off, light guy. I'll be furniture. Very brave, very quiet furniture.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Day nine. Even the plow guy came by, checked his blades, said 'see you on the other side of it' and drove off like that's a normal thing to say to a person.
It's so quiet now I can hear my own blood. The gulls are gone. The wind's going.
You've got your climb tonight. I've got my nerve to keep. Tell me the plan one more time. All of it, this time.

choices.text: (Lay out everything. Where she'll be. What you'll do.)
Trust me one more night. Then never again if you don't want to.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: Okay. Okay. I've got it.
Whatever happens up on that point — thank you for being the light that was on in a window that should've been dark.
That's how you found me. Funny that it works both ways. Go on. I'll see you on the other side of it.

nodes.speaker: june
lines: One more night. Fine. I've trusted you through worse sentences than that.
Go do the thing only you can do. I'll be here, minding the slack water, whatever that means. You said it once and I liked how scared you looked.
Come back, Wren. That was always the whole deal. You come back.

barks: (She's at the window, then she isn't.)
Knock the knock or I'm not opening.
Don't stand in the light out there.
You bring coffee or just yourself?
Two boxes done. Nineteen to go. Don't count them, it's rude.
The meter's spinning again. I made tea. That's it, I swear.

6: (A shape moves back from the glass.)
9: (A hand at the glass, then gone.)
